conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions.

First, let me review the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children under the age of 16, or children aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have three children actually - Alex, Blake, and Charlie. Alex is 4, Charlie is 15, and Blake is 16.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So you have three children: Alex (age 4), Charlie (age 15), and Blake (age 16).

To help assess your eligibility for each child, I need to ask a few more questions.

**Is Blake (your 16-year-old) currently in approved education or training?** (This includes things like A-levels, T-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs up to level 3, or home education that started before they turned 16)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake actually left school recently, but he's signed up with the careers service to figure out what to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know - Blake can still qualify for Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks after leaving education if he's registered with a government-sponsored careers service, which it sounds like he is.

Now, let me ask about your living situation: **Do all three children (Alex, Blake, and Charlie) live with you in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK, in Leeds actually. Alex and Charlie live with me at home, but Blake doesn't live with me - he lives elsewhere.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. So Alex and Charlie live with you in Leeds, but Blake lives elsewhere.

**Regarding Blake - even though he doesn't live with you, are you contributing financially towards his upkeep?** (This could include paying for things like food, clothes, pocket money, or contributing to his accommodation - and the contribution needs to be at least the same amount as Child Benefit, which is currently around £25 per week for the eldest child or £16.95 per week for additional children)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do help out with Blake. I contribute towards his upkeep - I help with clothes, food, that sort of thing.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's important for Blake's eligibility.

**Can you tell me approximately how much you contribute towards Blake's upkeep each week or month?** I need to understand if it's at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment (which would be around £25 per week for the eldest child or about £16.95 per week for other children).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute £50 a week towards Blake's upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's very helpful, thank you. £50 a week is well above the Child Benefit threshold, so that contribution should count towards your eligibility for Blake.

Now I need to check a few more things:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for any of your three children - Alex, Blake, or Charlie?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

**Are any of your children currently in hospital for more than 12 weeks, or in care for more than 8 weeks?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has been in hospital for a while now - about 3 months actually.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. I'm sorry to hear Alex has been in hospital. Three months is around 12-13 weeks.

**Since Alex has been in hospital for about 3 months, are you regularly spending money on Alex during this time?** (This could include buying things for them, visiting costs, or other expenses related to their care)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I still buy things for Alex - clothes, their favourite snacks, magazines, that sort of thing.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's important because it means you may still qualify for Child Benefit for Alex even though they've been in hospital for more than 12 weeks, since you're regularly spending money on them.

Just a few final questions:

**Are Alex, Blake, or Charlie working 24 hours or more a week, or receiving any benefits in their own name (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of them are working that much or getting any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. One last important question:

**Are you or Blake fostering any of these children, or are any of them in care with the local council providing payments towards their accommodation or maintenance?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of them are fostered and the council isn't paying anything towards them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Good News - You Are Eligible for Child Benefit! 

Based on the information you've provided, **you are eligible for Child Benefit for all three of your children: Alex, Blake, and Charlie.**

Here's the breakdown for each child:

**Alex (age 4):** ✓ Eligible
- Even though Alex has been in hospital for about 3 months (which is over the usual 12-week limit), you remain eligible because you're regularly spending money on Alex by buying clothes, snacks, magazines, and other items. The rules allow an exception to the 12-week hospital limit when you continue to regularly spend money on your child.

**Blake (age 16):** ✓ Eligible  
- Blake qualifies under the 20-week grace period for 16-17 year olds who have left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service. Additionally, even though Blake doesn't live with you, you're contributing £50 per week towards their upkeep, which is well above the Child Benefit amount and demonstrates you're responsible for Blake.

**Charlie (age 15):** ✓ Eligible
- Charlie lives with you, is under 16, and meets all the standard eligibility criteria.

Since no one else is claiming Child Benefit for any of your children, you can proceed with claiming for all three. Please note that only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, and you've confirmed no one else is currently claiming.

If you haven't already, you can apply for Child Benefit through the government website or by contacting HMRC.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 15 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 4 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for them. Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 3 months (12-13 weeks), which exceeds the 12-week threshold, you continue to regularly spend money on Alex by buying clothes, snacks, magazines and other items. According to the rules, there is an exception to the 12-week limit for children in UK hospitals if you regularly spend money on them. Therefore, you remain eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 16 years old and has recently left education but has registered with a government-sponsored careers service. According to the rules, you can claim Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Although Blake does not live with you, you are contributing £50 per week towards Blake's upkeep (including clothes and food), which is well above the Child Benefit threshold amount. Under the rules, you can be responsible for a child if you are paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 15 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for them. Charlie is not in hospital or care for extended periods, not working 24+ hours per week, and not receiving benefits in their own name. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for all three of your children. Alex (4) and Charlie (15) both live with you and meet the standard eligibility criteria, with Alex's hospital stay covered by the exception for regularly spending money on them. Blake (16) qualifies because you make sufficient financial contributions towards their upkeep (£50/week) and Blake is registered with the careers service within the 20-week grace period after leaving education. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for any of the children.
